news_summary: Scientists have made major breakthroughs in regrowing cartilage to reverse arthritis, uncovered unexpected connections between cancer and Alzheimer’s, and identified fasting as a potential way to combat gum disease, highlighting promising advances in health and disease prevention.
news_summary_fr: Les scientifiques ont réalisé des avancées majeures dans la régénération du cartilage pour lutter contre l'arthrite, mis au jour des liens inattendus entre le cancer et la maladie d'Alzheimer, et identifié le jeûne comme un moyen potentiel de lutter contre les maladies des gencives, soulignant ainsi des progrès prometteurs en matière de santé et de prévention des maladies.
news_summary_es: Los científicos han logrado avances significativos en la regeneración del cartílago para revertir la artritis, han descubierto vínculos inesperados entre el cáncer y el Alzheimer, y han identificado el ayuno como una posible forma de combatir la enfermedad de las encías, lo que pone de relieve avances prometedores en materia de salud y prevención de enfermedades.

articles.title: Stanford scientists regrow lost cartilage and reverse arthritis in major breakthrough
articles.summary: A new treatment that blocks an aging-related protein restored lost cartilage in old mice and helped prevent arthritis after knee injuries. Human cartilage samples showed similar signs of regeneration, raising hopes for a future drug that could repair joints instead of replacing them.
articles.content:
A treatment that targets a protein linked to aging has restored lost knee cartilage in older mice and prevented arthritis from developing after serious joint injuries, according to a Stanford Medicine-led study.

Researchers also found encouraging results in human tissue. Samples collected during knee replacement surgeries began producing new, functional cartilage when exposed to the treatment.

The findings raise the possibility that damaged cartilage caused by aging or osteoarthritis could one day be repaired with either a local injection or an oral medication. If successful in people, the approach could reduce the need for knee and hip replacement surgeries.

An oral version of the treatment is already being tested in clinical trials for age-related muscle weakness.

Targeting the Root Cause of Osteoarthritis

Osteoarthritis is the most common form of arthritis and affects about one in five adults in the United States. The disease gradually breaks down cartilage in the joints, causing pain, stiffness, and swelling. It is estimated to generate roughly $65 billion in direct health care costs each year.

Current treatments focus mainly on pain relief and, in severe cases, joint replacement surgery. No approved medication can slow, stop, or reverse the underlying disease process.

The new treatment works by blocking a protein called 15-PGDH, which researchers describe as a "gerozyme." This class of proteins becomes more abundant with age and contributes to declining tissue function throughout the body.

The same research team first identified gerozymes in 2023. Previous studies showed that 15-PGDH plays a major role in age-related muscle decline in mice. When researchers block the protein, older animals gain muscle mass and endurance. When the protein is artificially increased in young mice, their muscles become weaker and smaller.

Scientists have also linked 15-PGDH to the regeneration of bone, nerve, and blood cells.

A Different Type of Tissue Regeneration

In many tissues, regeneration occurs because stem cells multiply and develop into new specialized cells. Cartilage appears to work differently.

Instead of relying on stem cells, cartilage-producing cells called chondrocytes seem able to shift their gene activity and return to a more youthful state.

"This is a new way of regenerating adult tissue, and it has significant clinical promise for treating arthritis due to aging or injury," said Helen Blau, PhD, professor of microbiology and immunology. "We were looking for stem cells, but they are clearly not involved. It's very exciting."

Blau, director of the Baxter Laboratory for Stem Cell Biology and the Donald E. and Delia B. Baxter Foundation Professor, and Nidhi Bhutani, PhD, associate professor of orthopedic surgery, are the senior authors of the study, which was published in Science. Instructor of orthopedic surgery Mamta Singla, PhD, and former postdoctoral scholar Yu Xin (Will) Wang, PhD, are the lead authors. Wang is now an assistant professor at the Sanford Burnham Institute in San Diego.

Remarkable Cartilage Regrowth

"Millions of people suffer from joint pain and swelling as they age," Bhutani said. "It is a huge unmet medical need. Until now, there has been no drug that directly treats the cause of cartilage loss. But this gerozyme inhibitor causes a dramatic regeneration of cartilage beyond that reported in response to any other drug or intervention."

The human body contains three main forms of cartilage. Elastic cartilage provides flexibility in structures such as the outer ear. Fibrocartilage is tough and shock-absorbing, found in places like the discs between vertebrae. Hyaline cartilage is smooth and slippery, allowing joints such as the knees, hips, shoulders, and ankles to move freely.

Osteoarthritis primarily damages hyaline cartilage, also called articular cartilage.

As joints age or experience injury or excess stress from obesity, chondrocytes begin producing inflammatory molecules and breaking down collagen, the main structural component of cartilage. As collagen disappears, cartilage becomes thinner and softer. Inflammation then triggers the pain and swelling associated with osteoarthritis.

Unlike many other tissues, articular cartilage rarely repairs itself. While researchers have identified possible cartilage-producing stem cells in bone, similar cells have not been successfully identified in articular cartilage.

Why Researchers Focused on 15-PGDH

Earlier work from Blau's laboratory showed that prostaglandin E2 is critical for muscle stem cell function. The protein 15-PGDH breaks down prostaglandin E2.

When researchers inhibit 15-PGDH or increase prostaglandin E2 levels, damaged muscle, nerve, bone, colon, liver, and blood tissues regenerate more effectively in young mice.

The team wondered whether the same mechanism might influence cartilage aging.

When they compared cartilage from young and old mice, they found that levels of 15-PGDH approximately doubled with age.

To test the idea, researchers treated older mice with a small molecule drug that blocks 15-PGDH activity. Some animals received injections into the abdomen, exposing the whole body to the treatment. Others received injections directly into the knee joint.

Both approaches produced striking results. Cartilage that had become thinner and less functional with age grew thicker across the joint surface. Additional testing showed the regenerated tissue was hyaline cartilage, the type needed for healthy joint function, rather than the less effective fibrocartilage.

"Cartilage regeneration to such an extent in aged mice took us by surprise," Bhutani said. "The effect was remarkable."

Preventing Arthritis After ACL-Type Injuries

The researchers also investigated whether the treatment could protect joints after injury.

They used a mouse model that mimics ACL tears, a common sports injury seen in activities such as soccer, basketball, and skiing that involve sudden stopping, pivoting, or jumping.

Although ACL injuries can be surgically repaired, roughly half of affected people develop osteoarthritis in the injured joint within about 15 years.

Mice that received the gerozyme inhibitor twice weekly for four weeks after injury were far less likely to develop osteoarthritis. In contrast, untreated animals showed 15-PGDH levels that were about twice as high as those of uninjured mice and developed osteoarthritis within four weeks.

Treated mice also walked more normally and placed more weight on the injured limb.

"Interestingly, prostaglandin E2 has been implicated in inflammation and pain," Blau said. "But this research shows that, at normal biological levels, small increases in prostaglandin E2 can promote regeneration."

Reprogramming Aging Cartilage Cells

A closer look at cartilage cells revealed important differences between young and old joints.

Older chondrocytes were more likely to activate genes linked to inflammation and unwanted conversion of cartilage into bone. They were less likely to express genes associated with healthy cartilage formation.

The treatment appeared to reverse many of these age-related changes.

One group of chondrocytes that produced 15-PGDH and expressed genes involved in cartilage breakdown dropped from 8% of cells to 3% after treatment. Another group associated with fibrocartilage production fell from 16% to 8%.

Meanwhile, a population of cells involved in building hyaline cartilage and maintaining the extracellular matrix increased from 22% to 42%.

Overall, the results suggest the treatment shifts cartilage toward a younger, healthier state without requiring stem or progenitor cells.

Human Cartilage Also Responded

The team then examined cartilage removed from people undergoing total knee replacement surgery for osteoarthritis.

After one week of treatment with the 15-PGDH inhibitor, the tissue showed fewer cartilage-degrading cells and lower activity of genes linked to cartilage breakdown and fibrocartilage production. The samples also began generating new articular cartilage.

"The mechanism is quite striking and really shifted our perspective about how tissue regeneration can occur," Bhutani said. "It's clear that a large pool of already existing cells in cartilage are changing their gene expression patterns. And by targeting these cells for regeneration, we may have an opportunity to have a bigger overall impact clinically."

Blau added, "Phase 1 clinical trials of a 15-PGDH inhibitor for muscle weakness have shown that it is safe and active in healthy volunteers. Our hope is that a similar trial will be launched soon to test its effect in cartilage regeneration. We are very excited about this potential breakthrough. Imagine regrowing existing cartilage and avoiding joint replacement."

Researchers from the Sanford Burnham Prebys Medical Discovery Institute also contributed to the study.

The research was funded by the National Institutes of Health (grants R01AR070864, R01AR077530, R01AG069858 and R00NS120278), the Baxter Foundation for Stem Cell Biology, the Li Ka Shing Foundation, the Stanford Cardiovascular Institute, the Milky Way Research Foundation, the Canadian Institutes of Health Research, a Stanford Translational Research and Applied Medicine Pilot grant, a GlaxoSmithKline Sir James Black Postdoctoral Fellowship, and a Stanford Dean's Postdoctoral Fellowship.

Blau, Bhutani, and several co-authors are inventors on Stanford University patent applications involving 15-PGDH inhibition for cartilage repair and tissue rejuvenation that have been licensed to Epirium Bio. Blau is a co-founder of Myoforte/Epirium and holds equity and stock options in the company.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a major scientific breakthrough with a new treatment that regenerates lost cartilage and reverses arthritis in aged mice and human tissue samples, offering hope for a future drug that could reduce or eliminate the need for joint replacement surgeries. The treatment targets a protein linked to aging, showing broad potential impact for millions suffering from osteoarthritis, a common and costly disease. The study is detailed, focused on a single significant advancement, and includes clinical trial progress, indicating substantial real-world benefits.
articles.personality_title: Stanford scientists develop treatment that regrows cartilage and fights arthritis
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Osteoarthritis is a common disease that damages the smooth cartilage in joints like knees and hips. This damage causes pain and stiffness, and currently, there is no medicine that can repair the cartilage or stop the disease. People often need joint replacement surgery when the cartilage is badly damaged.

**What happened** – Scientists at Stanford found a new treatment that blocks a protein called 15-PGDH, which increases with age and harms cartilage. In older mice, this treatment helped regrow lost knee cartilage and prevented arthritis after knee injuries. The same treatment also worked on human cartilage samples taken during knee surgeries, showing signs of new cartilage growth.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows that cartilage, which usually cannot repair itself, can be made to grow again by changing how cartilage cells behave. The treatment could lead to drugs that fix damaged joints without surgery, helping millions of people with arthritis. It also works by making old cartilage cells act younger rather than needing special stem cells.

**What's next step** – The treatment is already being tested in people for muscle weakness, and researchers hope to start trials soon to see if it can safely regrow cartilage in patients. If successful, this could reduce the number of knee and hip replacement surgeries and improve quality of life for many.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new drug blocking an age-related protein has been shown to regrow knee cartilage and prevent arthritis in mice and human tissue, pointing to future treatments that could repair joints without surgery.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques de Stanford développent un traitement qui régénère le cartilage et combat l’arthrite
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’arthrose est une maladie courante qui endommage le cartilage lisse des articulations comme les genoux et les hanches. Cette détérioration cause douleur et raideur, et il n’existe actuellement aucun médicament capable de réparer le cartilage ou d’arrêter la maladie. Les personnes doivent souvent subir une chirurgie de remplacement articulaire lorsque le cartilage est très abîmé.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques de Stanford ont découvert un nouveau traitement qui bloque une protéine appelée 15-PGDH, qui augmente avec l’âge et nuit au cartilage. Chez des souris âgées, ce traitement a permis de régénérer le cartilage du genou perdu et d’empêcher l’arthrite après des blessures au genou. Le même traitement a aussi fonctionné sur des échantillons de cartilage humain prélevés lors de chirurgies, montrant des signes de nouvelle croissance du cartilage.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre que le cartilage, qui ne se répare généralement pas tout seul, peut repousser en modifiant le comportement des cellules du cartilage. Le traitement pourrait mener à des médicaments qui réparent les articulations abîmées sans chirurgie, aidant des millions de personnes atteintes d’arthrite. Il agit en rendant les cellules du cartilage âgées plus jeunes, sans besoin de cellules souches.

**Prochaine étape** – Le traitement est déjà testé chez l’humain pour la faiblesse musculaire, et les chercheurs espèrent bientôt lancer des essais pour vérifier s’il peut régénérer le cartilage en toute sécurité chez les patients. En cas de succès, cela pourrait réduire le nombre de chirurgies de remplacement articulaire et améliorer la qualité de vie.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un nouveau médicament bloquant une protéine liée à l’âge a permis de régénérer le cartilage du genou et d’empêcher l’arthrite chez la souris et sur du tissu humain, ouvrant la voie à des traitements futurs réparant les articulations sans chirurgie.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos de Stanford desarrollan tratamiento que regenera cartílago y combate la artritis
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La osteoartritis es una enfermedad común que daña el cartílago suave de las articulaciones como las rodillas y caderas. Este daño causa dolor y rigidez, y actualmente no hay medicamentos que puedan reparar el cartílago o detener la enfermedad. Muchas personas necesitan cirugía para reemplazar la articulación cuando el cartílago está muy dañado.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos de Stanford encontraron un nuevo tratamiento que bloquea una proteína llamada 15-PGDH, que aumenta con la edad y daña el cartílago. En ratones viejos, este tratamiento ayudó a regenerar el cartílago perdido en las rodillas y previno la artritis después de lesiones. El mismo tratamiento también funcionó en muestras de cartílago humano tomadas durante cirugías de rodilla, mostrando signos de crecimiento nuevo.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque muestra que el cartílago, que normalmente no se repara solo, puede crecer de nuevo al cambiar cómo se comportan las células del cartílago. El tratamiento podría llevar a medicamentos que reparen articulaciones dañadas sin cirugía, ayudando a millones con artritis. Funciona haciendo que las células viejas del cartílago actúen como si fueran jóvenes, sin necesidad de células madre.

**Próximo paso** – El tratamiento ya se está probando en personas para debilidad muscular, y los investigadores esperan comenzar pronto ensayos para ver si puede regenerar cartílago de forma segura en pacientes. Si tiene éxito, podría reducir las cirugías de reemplazo de rodilla y cadera y mejorar la vida de muchas personas.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Un nuevo medicamento que bloquea una proteína relacionada con la edad ha demostrado regenerar cartílago de rodilla y prevenir artritis en ratones y tejido humano, señalando tratamientos futuros que podrían reparar articulaciones sin cirugía.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of an ancient, weathered knee joint made of smooth, translucent cartilage glowing softly with renewed thickness and vitality, surrounded by delicate, stylized molecular structures symbolizing protein blockers gently dissolving cracked, brittle cartilage fragments into vibrant new hyaline cartilage layers, all rendered in natural earth tones and soft blues.

articles.title: Scientists discover a surprising cancer link to Alzheimer’s disease
articles.summary: Researchers discovered that mutations linked to blood cancers may help trigger Alzheimer’s disease by creating overly inflammatory immune cells in the brain. The unexpected finding could lead to new blood-based screening methods and potential treatments borrowed from cancer medicine.
articles.content:
As people grow older, their cells steadily pick up new genetic mutations. A study from Boston Children's Hospital, published in Cell, has uncovered an unexpected twist in that process. Researchers found that microglia, the immune cells that reside in the brain, accumulate mutations in specific cancer-driving genes. Rather than causing cancer, however, these mutations may contribute to the development of Alzheimer's disease.

The research was led by Christopher Walsh, MD, PhD, Chief of the Division of Genetics and Genomics at Boston Children's Hospital and an Investigator of the Howard Hughes Medical Institute. Collaborators included Alice Eunjung Lee, PhD, and August Yue Huang, PhD, also of the Division of Genetics and Genomics. All three are Professors at Harvard Medical School and Associate Members of the Broad Institute of MIT and Harvard.

The team says the findings could point to new ways to diagnose and treat Alzheimer's disease.

"We find that to some extent, Alzheimer's disease is a little like cancer -- driven by the same mutations that drive blood cancers like lymphoma and leukemia," said Walsh. "This is helpful because we have a lot of drugs to fight cancer and some of them might be useful therapeutically for Alzheimer's disease."

Cancer Driver Mutations Found in Alzheimer's Brains

To investigate, researchers analyzed 149 cancer-driving genes in brain tissue samples from 190 people with Alzheimer's disease and compared them with samples from 121 healthy brains.

The Alzheimer's samples contained more single-letter DNA changes than the healthy tissue. Many of these alterations repeatedly appeared in the same five cancer driver genes, suggesting that microglia were accumulating mutations in a specific set of genes.

Microglia serve as the brain's cleanup crew. These cells remove debris and help eliminate infected, damaged, or dying cells. Scientists had long believed that microglia remain confined to the brain and do not cross the blood brain barrier, unlike many other immune cells that circulate through the bloodstream.

Unexpected Link Between Blood Cells and the Brain

The mutations identified in microglia are commonly associated with blood cancers. That observation prompted the researchers to look for the same mutations in blood samples from people with Alzheimer's disease.

They did not expect to find them.

Instead, the blood cells from the same Alzheimer's patients carried the identical cancer-associated mutations.

"It was actually a really unexpected finding that suggests a totally new mechanism for Alzheimer's disease pathogenesis," said Huang. "The findings mean that the blood's immune cells with cancer mutations are likely getting into the brain and contributing to disease."

How Mutant Immune Cells May Fuel Alzheimer's

The researchers propose that aging or injury can weaken the blood-brain barrier, allowing immune cells from the bloodstream to enter the brain. Once there, these cells may transform into microglia-like cells.

At the same time, protein clumps that build up in the brain trigger microglia to multiply and respond. Cells that possess a biological advantage are more likely to expand, including the microglia-like cells carrying cancer-related mutations.

According to the researchers, these mutated cells may create a more inflammatory and damaging environment than healthy microglia. As a result, nearby neurons can be harmed and die, contributing to the progression of Alzheimer's disease.

Potential for New Alzheimer's Tests and Treatments

The discovery could eventually lead to new approaches for detecting Alzheimer's risk.

"Because it's hard to access brain tissue in a living patient, genetic screens using blood samples could be developed to test whether a person carries these mutations, and has an increased risk of developing Alzheimer's disease," said Lee.

In a follow-up study posted as a preprint on bioRxiv, Huang and Lee found additional evidence supporting the connection. Their analysis showed that cancer driver mutations detected in blood samples increased Alzheimer's disease risk independently of APOE4, a well-established genetic risk factor for the disease.

The research was conducted in collaboration with the Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai. Funding was provided by the Howard Hughes Medical Institute, the National Institute on Aging, the NIH Common Fund through the Somatic Mosaicism Across Human Tissues (SMaHT) consortium, and the Suh Kyungbae Foundation (SUHF).
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery linking cancer-related mutations to Alzheimer's disease, revealing a new mechanism that could lead to blood-based screening and novel treatments. This breakthrough has broad implications for millions affected by Alzheimer's and represents meaningful progress in medical research with potential large-scale health benefits.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find cancer gene mutations linked to Alzheimer’s disease
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – As people age, their cells collect genetic changes. Scientists have been studying how these changes might cause diseases like cancer. Now, a new study shows these changes may also play a role in Alzheimer’s disease, a brain condition that affects memory and thinking.

**What happened** – Researchers at Boston Children's Hospital studied brain and blood samples from hundreds of people with Alzheimer’s. They found that certain mutations, often seen in blood cancers like leukemia, also appear in immune cells in the brain called microglia. Surprisingly, these same mutations were found in the blood cells of Alzheimer’s patients, suggesting that mutated blood cells might enter the brain and cause problems.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows Alzheimer’s disease might partly develop from similar genetic changes that cause some cancers. It changes how scientists think about the disease and opens up new possibilities for testing and treatment. For example, doctors might one day use simple blood tests to detect these mutations and identify people at risk for Alzheimer’s. Also, some cancer drugs might help treat Alzheimer’s by targeting these mutated cells.

**What’s next step** – Scientists plan to study how these mutated immune cells affect the brain over time and test whether cancer medicines can slow down or stop Alzheimer’s symptoms. They will also work on developing blood tests for early detection, which could make it easier to find and treat Alzheimer’s earlier.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Researchers discovered that blood cancer-related mutations in immune cells may help cause Alzheimer’s disease, pointing to new ways to detect and treat it using blood tests and existing cancer drugs.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent un lien entre des mutations liées au cancer et la maladie d’Alzheimer
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En vieillissant, les cellules accumulent des changements génétiques. Les scientifiques étudient comment ces changements peuvent provoquer des maladies comme le cancer. Une nouvelle étude montre que ces mutations pourraient aussi jouer un rôle dans la maladie d’Alzheimer, une maladie du cerveau qui affecte la mémoire et la réflexion.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’hôpital pour enfants de Boston ont analysé des échantillons de cerveau et de sang de centaines de personnes atteintes d’Alzheimer. Ils ont découvert que certaines mutations, souvent présentes dans les cancers du sang comme la leucémie, se retrouvent aussi dans des cellules immunitaires du cerveau appelées microglies. Étonnamment, ces mêmes mutations étaient dans les cellules sanguines des patients, ce qui laisse penser que des cellules sanguines mutées pourraient pénétrer dans le cerveau et causer des dégâts.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre que la maladie d’Alzheimer pourrait être en partie causée par les mêmes mutations que certains cancers. Cela change la façon dont les scientifiques comprennent la maladie et ouvre de nouvelles possibilités de tests et de traitements. Par exemple, les médecins pourraient un jour utiliser des tests sanguins simples pour détecter ces mutations et identifier les personnes à risque. De plus, certains médicaments contre le cancer pourraient aider à traiter Alzheimer en ciblant ces cellules mutées.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont étudier comment ces cellules immunitaires mutées affectent le cerveau au fil du temps et tester si les médicaments contre le cancer peuvent ralentir ou arrêter les symptômes d’Alzheimer. Ils travailleront aussi à développer des tests sanguins pour une détection précoce, facilitant ainsi le diagnostic et le traitement.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des chercheurs ont découvert que des mutations liées au cancer dans des cellules immunitaires sanguines pourraient contribuer à la maladie d’Alzheimer, ouvrant la voie à des tests sanguins et à des traitements issus de la médecine du cancer.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos encuentran mutaciones relacionadas con el cáncer vinculadas a la enfermedad de Alzheimer
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Al envejecer, las células acumulan cambios genéticos. Los científicos han estudiado cómo estos cambios pueden causar enfermedades como el cáncer. Ahora, un nuevo estudio muestra que estas mutaciones también podrían estar involucradas en la enfermedad de Alzheimer, una condición cerebral que afecta la memoria y el pensamiento.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores del Hospital Infantil de Boston estudiaron muestras de cerebro y sangre de cientos de personas con Alzheimer. Encontraron que ciertas mutaciones, que suelen aparecer en cánceres de la sangre como la leucemia, también están presentes en células inmunitarias del cerebro llamadas microglías. Sorprendentemente, estas mismas mutaciones se encontraron en las células sanguíneas de pacientes con Alzheimer, lo que sugiere que las células sanguíneas mutadas podrían entrar al cerebro y causar daños.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque muestra que la enfermedad de Alzheimer podría desarrollarse en parte por cambios genéticos similares a los que causan algunos tipos de cáncer. Esto cambia la forma en que los científicos entienden la enfermedad y abre nuevas posibilidades para pruebas y tratamientos. Por ejemplo, los médicos podrían usar análisis de sangre para detectar estas mutaciones y saber quién tiene riesgo de Alzheimer. Además, algunos medicamentos contra el cáncer podrían ayudar a tratar la enfermedad al atacar estas células mutadas.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean estudiar cómo estas células inmunitarias mutadas afectan el cerebro con el tiempo y probar si los medicamentos contra el cáncer pueden frenar o detener los síntomas de Alzheimer. También trabajarán en desarrollar pruebas de sangre para una detección temprana, facilitando el diagnóstico y tratamiento.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los investigadores encontraron que mutaciones relacionadas con el cáncer en células inmunitarias sanguíneas pueden contribuir a la enfermedad de Alzheimer, lo que abre nuevas formas de detectarla y tratarla con análisis de sangre y medicamentos contra el cáncer.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting showing a stylized human brain softly illuminated from within, with glowing clusters of small, friendly-looking immune cells (microglia) depicted as gentle, radiant orbs carrying tiny, distinct strands of DNA; nearby, a translucent barrier symbolizing the blood-brain barrier is partially open, allowing similar glowing immune cells from a flowing bloodstream to enter and merge, illustrating the unexpected link between blood cancer mutations and Alzheimer's disease.

articles.title: Podcast Transcript June 12th, 2026— From a 340-year slavery law finally repealed to pancakes for cigarette butts: 10 solutions this week
articles.summary:
Episode Description: A high-risk breast cancer diagnosis used to mean one thing: chemotherapy. A large UK trial just found that 68 percent of those patients could skip it safely, based on a genomic test that already exists and is already in use. Arielle and Karissa also get into the pigeon liver finding that stumped scientists […]
The post Podcast Transcript June 12th, 2026— From a 340-year slavery law finally repealed to pancakes for cigarette butts: 10 solutions this week first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
Episode Description:

A high-risk breast cancer diagnosis used to mean one thing: chemotherapy. A large UK trial just found that 68 percent of those patients could skip it safely, based on a genomic test that already exists and is already in use.

Arielle and Karissa also get into the pigeon liver finding that stumped scientists for over a century, a California city that voted to permanently ban data centers, and France formally repealing a law that had been sitting on the books since 1685.

Stay tuned until the end for this week’s Emissary Shout Out!

If you have questions, comments, feedback, suggestions, or just want to say hi, send a message to: podcast@optimistdaily.com.

Want to be part of the Optimism Movement? Become an Emissary.

Subscribe to our FREE Daily/Weekly Newsletter and follow us on Instagram, X, and Blue Sky.

The Optimist Daily is a project of the World Business Academy.

Donate link: https://www.optimistdaily.com/donate-to-support-the-optimist-daily/?gift=Y%20http://

Theme and all original music by Marvin Lanes

Transcript:

Karissa:

Hello everyone , I’m Karissa.

Arielle:

And I’m Arielle.

Karissa:

And welcome back to The Optimist Daily’s weekly roundup

Arielle:

Yes, we are so happy to be here, feeling really positive today personally. How are you, Karissa?

Karissa:

Yeah, I’m feeling pretty positive myself. The sun is shining, lots of good news to share.

Arielle:

And we also have two Emissary shout-outs that came in.

Karissa:

Yeah, keep the shout-outs coming, Emissaries. We wanna recognize what makes you optimistic, of course. And if you want a shout-out on the podcast and support The Optimist Daily financially, you can become an Emissary, and the link is in the show notes.

Arielle:

Yes, we cannot do this work without you, so thank you so much to all the Emissaries out there. But also thanks to all the Optimists who are just part of our community who read our articles or listen to the podcast or both, follow us on socials. All of that support, rates, reviews, subscriptions, all of that really does keep us going, and yeah, we definitely need it. Uh, some days are not as positive as others, and knowing that we have such a great community around us helps us continue on.

Karissa:

If you want These solutions in a different format, we have our daily and weekly edition of the newsletter that you can sign up for.

And again, link is in the show notes.

Arielle:

And if you have any comments, questions, or feedback, you can reach us at podcast@optimistdaily.com.

Karissa:

Yeah, or on any social media channels. We’re @optimistdaily on just about everything except on X, where we are @OdeToOptimism.

Arielle:

Oh, I should mention that next week there is a very, very special guest interview, coming out.

Karissa:

That’s right. I am looking forward to hearing this special guest episode.

Arielle:

Yes. But for now, let’s focus on the solutions that were published this week on The Optimist Daily. Karissa, do you wanna jump in?

Karissa:

Yeah!

The first solution we shared this week is called “Breast cancer genomic test could spare millions from chemotherapy.” A massive UK trial just found that 68% of high-risk breast cancer patients could safely skip chemotherapy based on a single genomic test. But the key word here is high risk. These are patients who, by every traditional measure, were supposed to need chemotherapy. Chemotherapy is just a very intensive and emotional process to go through,

Arielle:

Mm-hmm.

Karissa:

of course, physically tolling as well.

So the fact that this test could possibly change clinical treatment of breast cancer is very optimistic in my opinion.

Arielle:

Yeah, it’s great to see the growing awareness around, long-term quality of life costs of cancer treatment and not just survival outcomes. This genomic test, uh, Prosig- Prosigna I think it’s called, already exists and is available. So this isn’t a future technology story.

It’s a clinical practice story that’s happening right now.

Karissa:

In this trial, 68% of patients who were clinically flagged high risk scored low on the genomic test, and it meant that their outcomes were nearly identical to those who went through full chemotherapy. Karissa:

I’m sure it’s definitely in the works.

All right, I’m gonna move on to solution number two, which is “The parenting habit that builds lifelong closeness with adult children.”

The research is fairly consistent. Some parents stay genuinely close to their adult children, and some don’t, of course. But the gap isn’t really about love, because most parents love their children deeply.

In this solution, we get some insight from a Harvard-affiliated psychologist who has been studying what actually separates the two groups, and it comes down to one pattern that plays out in small moments across years. And the core finding was that unconditional parental regard: love, and acceptance that doesn’t depend on the child meeting expectations, is the defining factor in whether adult children stay genuinely close to their parents.

Yeah, parent-child relationships are often very complex and, this article goes into what, um, conditional parental regard looks like. I also thought this solution made a good point that a lot of parenting advice is aimed at parents with younger children or teenage children, but, you know, you’re always gonna be a parent through your entire life,

parenting is a life journey.

Um, and I think it definitely helps me stay close to them ’cause, yeah, it’s so remarkable when a parent, a person of authority, can admit when they’re wrong and, um, yeah, for it not to be, like, something that they’ll never do or something that you can never hope for as a child. Um, so yeah, I would say it’s good.

Yeah, exactly. I think I’m still waiting For a couple apologies. But one day. We’re all evolving.

So France’s National Assembly just voted 254 to zero to formally repeal the Code Noir, and it was the 1685 royal decree from Louis XIV that governed enslaved people in French colonies. I would like to say that it’s opening up this conversation of, I mean, for one, why was it still there?

What can France do to give proper acknowledgement and reparations to what they have done throughout history.

Mm-hmm. We have another lifestyle solution this week, and it’s “five habits that separate growing teams from stagnant ones.”

Knowledge workers lose 60% of their working time to what researchers call work about work, and that’s status updates, unfocused meetings, tool switching that produces nothing.

But a chief people officer at a major firm says the gap between teams that keep growing and teams that just plateau isn’t talent. It’s daily habits, and habits, unlike talent, are entirely within your control. So this article gave some good insight into, yeah, just how to keep growing a team. The advice comes from Julie Turpin, who is the chief people officer at Brown and Brown, and she says results that stick are built by habits that stick over time. The most Interesting habit to me, was to question inherited habits that happen on a team.

Yeah, exactly. There’s four other Habits in here that Julie shares, so check them out if you are a team leader or part of a team.

The last solution that I have to share today is that “Monterey Park became the first US city to permanently ban data centers.”

Monterey Park is in California, and it just became the first city in the US to permanently ban data centers with 86% of the vote this recent election. The ballot resolution here cited noise pollution, air pollution, and spikes in electricity and water rates as the driving reasons of why.

This serves as a good model for other US cities because data centers are rising up everywhere with the rise of AI, and they have pretty negative effects.

As Mayor Elizabeth Yang told Politico, “A lot of other cities that are facing data center proposals are going to follow suit,” pointing to data center protests already happening nationwide.

And as you said, data centers do have a pretty negative impact, not just environmentally, but also for the residents near them because

residents have documented real impacts on utility bills for one, which are already expensive enough, let’s not spike them any higher, and their quality of life.

Yeah, exactly. But for now, this serves as just a great model that, this can be, you know, a community voted on issue, and other cities and areas may follow a similar process and put this on their ballots.

Arielle:

I will continue with the sixth solution, which is titled: “How the act of learning to read rewires the brain and changes the way you hear.” Adults who learn to read and adults who never did can both understand their native languages perfectly.

But if you put them in front of an unfamiliar language, something very different happens, and the gap has nothing to do with intelligence. It’s about what reading instruction does to the brain at the structural level. So this study was published in Cortex, and it was led by cognitive neuroscientist Mariana P. Nucci at the University of São Paulo. They tested three groups: 23 young educated adults, 21 older educated adults, and 15 functionally illiterate older adults, all native Portuguese speakers. They had two listening tasks. They listened to Portuguese, which is their native language, and Japanese, which is completely unfamiliar. In the Portuguese task, all three groups performed similarly, around 90% accuracy, so no meaningful difference between groups. But the Japanese task revealed a stark split.

Functionally illiterate participants scored 17% accuracy, older educated adults 48%, and young educated adults 75%. So the brain imaging showed educated participants activated the right inferior frontal gyrus during the Japanese task. That’s the right hemisphere counterpart to Broca’s area associated with language processing.

And functionally illiterate participants showed no activation in this region at all. I think this is really fascinating. Basically, it’s just a great motivation to make sure that your kids are learning to read well. Um, there’s also a debate over how to teach reading, if it’s, you know, phonetics versus whole language, and that has also been politic-politically charged in recent years.

So this research is structural evidence for what phonics instruction is actually building in the brain.

Yeah, exactly. This is just such a fascinating study here and fascinating finding

Yeah, I kind of went into detail there, but this was… Yeah, I couldn’t stop reading about it, so I highly recommend checking it out.

from the week.

A marine scientist in California is rebuilding them using shells scraped off restaurant plates, 24,000 pounds collected so far and counting. Yeah, that’s pretty fascinating.

Yeah, exactly. Restaurant leftovers becoming coastal infrastructure is just very compelling.

Mm-hmm.

to me. I don’t eat oysters, but I’ve definitely been with people who do, and just I’ve never thought about what happens to the shells, and the fact that they can be repurposed for something so important is amazing.

Scientists have been trying to locate the magnetic compass inside homing pigeons for over a century. They looked in the beak, they looked in the eyes, they looked in the brain.

It’s,

articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a major UK clinical trial showing that 68% of high-risk breast cancer patients can safely avoid chemotherapy using an existing genomic test, which has significant positive real-world impact on patient quality of life and treatment protocols. This breakthrough affects a broad population and is well-detailed, making it genuinely inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: New UK study shows most high-risk breast cancer patients can avoid chemotherapy safely
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Traditionally, a high-risk breast cancer diagnosis almost always meant patients had to undergo chemotherapy, a tough and exhausting treatment. However, a genomic test called Prosigna, already available, offers new hope by better predicting who really needs chemotherapy.

**What happened** – A large clinical trial in the UK found that 68% of high-risk breast cancer patients could skip chemotherapy safely based on their Prosigna genomic test results. These patients, previously thought to require chemotherapy, had similar health outcomes without it.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because chemotherapy can cause many side effects and emotional stress. Avoiding it when not needed improves patients’ quality of life and reduces unnecessary medical treatments. Since the test already exists, this change can happen quickly in hospitals, helping millions.

**What's next step** – Medical teams are encouraged to adopt the genomic test more widely to guide treatment decisions. Researchers may also explore if similar tests can help with other types of cancer, potentially changing cancer care even further.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A widely available genomic test now helps most high-risk breast cancer patients skip chemotherapy safely, improving treatment and quality of life.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une étude britannique montre que la majorité des patientes à haut risque de cancer du sein peuvent éviter la chimiothérapie en toute sécurité
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Jusqu’à présent, un diagnostic de cancer du sein à haut risque signifiait presque toujours une chimiothérapie, un traitement lourd et éprouvant. Cependant, un test génomique appelé Prosigna, déjà disponible, offre un nouvel espoir en identifiant mieux qui a réellement besoin de ce traitement.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Un grand essai clinique au Royaume-Uni a révélé que 68 % des patientes à haut risque pouvaient éviter la chimiothérapie en toute sécurité grâce aux résultats de leur test génomique Prosigna. Ces patientes, auparavant censées nécessiter une chimiothérapie, ont eu des résultats de santé similaires sans ce traitement.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car la chimiothérapie provoque de nombreux effets secondaires et un stress émotionnel. L’éviter lorsqu’elle n’est pas nécessaire améliore la qualité de vie des patientes et réduit les traitements médicaux inutiles. Comme le test existe déjà, ce changement peut rapidement être appliqué dans les hôpitaux, aidant ainsi des millions de personnes.

**Prochaine étape** – Les équipes médicales sont invitées à utiliser plus largement ce test génomique pour guider les décisions de traitement. Les chercheurs pourraient aussi étudier si des tests similaires peuvent aider pour d’autres types de cancer, modifiant encore plus les soins.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un test génomique largement disponible permet désormais à la plupart des patientes à haut risque de cancer du sein d’éviter la chimiothérapie en toute sécurité, améliorant ainsi le traitement et la qualité de vie.
articles.personality_title_es: Un estudio en Reino Unido muestra que la mayoría de pacientes con cáncer de mama de alto riesgo pueden evitar la quimioterapia de forma segura
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Tradicionalmente, un diagnóstico de cáncer de mama de alto riesgo significaba casi siempre que los pacientes debían someterse a quimioterapia, un tratamiento difícil y agotador. Sin embargo, una prueba genómica llamada Prosigna, ya disponible, ofrece una nueva esperanza al predecir mejor quién realmente necesita quimioterapia.

**Qué pasó** – Un gran ensayo clínico en Reino Unido encontró que el 68% de los pacientes con cáncer de mama de alto riesgo podían evitar la quimioterapia de forma segura según los resultados de su prueba genómica Prosigna. Estos pacientes, antes considerados necesitados de quimioterapia, tuvieron resultados de salud similares sin ella.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque la quimioterapia puede causar muchos efectos secundarios y estrés emocional. Evitarla cuando no es necesaria mejora la calidad de vida de los pacientes y reduce tratamientos médicos innecesarios. Como la prueba ya existe, este cambio puede implementarse rápidamente en los hospitales, ayudando a millones.

**Próximo paso** – Se anima a los equipos médicos a adoptar la prueba genómica más ampliamente para guiar las decisiones de tratamiento. Los investigadores también podrían explorar si pruebas similares pueden ayudar con otros tipos de cáncer, cambiando aún más la atención médica.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una prueba genómica disponible ayuda ahora a la mayoría de pacientes con cáncer de mama de alto riesgo a evitar la quimioterapia de forma segura, mejorando el tratamiento y la calidad de vida.
articles.image_prompt: A serene and hopeful scene of a glowing genomic DNA strand gently cradled by delicate hands made of soft light, beside a vibrant orange peel spiraling upward like a protective vine around a cluster of tiny oyster shells nestled in calm coastal waters, all rendered in warm, natural earth tones with a softly textured, painterly style.

articles.title: Can fasting fight gum disease? Scientists find surprising link
articles.summary: A low-calorie fasting-style diet significantly reduced inflammation linked to gum disease in a small clinical study. The findings suggest that what people eat may influence gum health almost as much as what they do with a toothbrush.
articles.content:
People who follow a short-term low-calorie diet may experience lower levels of inflammation associated with gum disease, according to new research from King's College London.

The study suggests that lifestyle changes could play an important role alongside plaque removal and good oral hygiene in managing gum disease.

Although fasting has previously been linked to reduced inflammation throughout the body, this is the first study to show a connection between fasting and markers of gum disease. The findings add to growing evidence that oral health and overall health are closely connected.

Dr. Giuseppe Mainas, first author of the study from King's College London, said: "Our study suggests lifestyle modifications could be important alongside proper tooth brushing for patients."

Gum Disease and Diet

Periodontitis is a severe form of gum disease that affects millions of people worldwide. The condition has also been associated with other serious health problems, including heart disease and diabetes.

Current treatment typically focuses on cleaning infected areas around the teeth and controlling plaque buildup. However, researchers are increasingly investigating whether dietary changes could help improve treatment outcomes.

To explore this possibility, the researchers recruited 28 patients from hospitals across Spain. Participants were divided into two groups: one followed a five-day calorie-restricted diet, while the other continued eating as usual.

Those in the fasting group consumed 1,100 calories per day for the first two days, followed by 750 calories per day for the next three days. On the sixth day, calories were gradually increased using soft foods before participants returned to their normal diets on the seventh day. This cycle was repeated three times over a six-month period, and participants reported that the diet was relatively easy to follow.

Reduced Inflammation in Blood and Gum Tissue

After six months, researchers analyzed samples taken from participants' blood and gingival crevicular fluid, a liquid found in the small space between the tooth and gum that helps protect the gums and defend against harmful microbes.

Compared with the control group, participants who completed the fasting program showed lower levels of inflammation-related markers in both their blood and gum tissue. They also had lower levels of C-reactive protein, a widely used marker of inflammation throughout the body.

In addition, the fasting group showed reductions in molecules specifically associated with inflammation in the gums.

Why Might Fasting Help?

Senior author Prof Luigi Nibali of King's College London said: "There may be multiple reasons why fasting is beneficial to gum disease patients. Fasting reduces oxidative stress in the body, a common cause of inflammation, which can damage cells and DNA.

"Intake of high calorific foods and refined carbohydrates, for example in cakes and biscuits, can also cause inflammation -- so restricting these foods also reduces oxidative stress in the body.

"It may also be that fasting has beneficial effects on the microbiome -- the body's community of bacteria that help to keep it healthy. However, further research is needed to confirm this relationship."

The researchers say fasting-mimicking diets could eventually be studied as an additional tool to support standard gum disease treatments, including professional dental cleaning and oral hygiene guidance.

Future Research and Treatment Potential

Dr. Mainas added: "Now we have established this relationship, we would like to do a larger study, before potentially incorporating into gum disease treatment in the future. There may be patients where restricting foods can be dangerous, such as those with diabetes, so the advice will need to be targeted to specific patient groups. We are currently investigating how we could implement these benefits in high-risk groups who may not be able to fast."

The new findings build on years of research at King's College London examining the links between oral health and overall health. Last year, researchers at King's found that following the mediterranean diet can reduce gum disease and that successful dental treatment can lower the risk of diabetes and heart disease.

The study was published in the Journal of Clinical Periodontology (JCP).

Funding for the research was provided through a Medical Research Council-Impact Accelerator Account (MRC-IAA) grant.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a meaningful scientific study showing that a low-calorie fasting diet can reduce inflammation linked to gum disease, a condition affecting millions globally. This finding has broad health implications, suggesting a new, accessible lifestyle approach to managing gum disease alongside traditional treatments. The study is well-detailed, presenting clinical evidence and potential mechanisms, and it points toward future research and practical applications, making it a substantive and significant health breakthrough.
articles.personality_title: Fasting diet shown to reduce inflammation linked to gum disease
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Gum disease, especially a serious type called periodontitis, affects millions of people worldwide. It causes inflammation in the gums and can lead to other health problems like heart disease and diabetes. Usual treatments focus on cleaning teeth and removing plaque, but scientists wanted to see if diet could also help.

**What happened** – Researchers at King's College London studied 28 patients in Spain. Half followed a low-calorie fasting-style diet for five days, repeated three times over six months. They ate fewer calories than usual—starting with 1,100 calories per day for two days, then 750 calories for three days. The other half ate normally. After six months, the fasting group showed lower levels of inflammation in their blood and gums compared to the regular diet group.

**Impact** – This study is the first to show that fasting can reduce inflammation linked specifically to gum disease. It suggests that what people eat may be almost as important as brushing their teeth when it comes to gum health. The diet lowered markers of inflammation, which could help protect gums and reduce the risk of serious health problems connected to gum disease.

**What’s next step** – Researchers want to do larger studies to confirm these results and figure out how to use fasting diets safely, especially for people who might be at risk, like those with diabetes. They hope to explore how fasting could be added to regular gum disease treatments like dental cleaning and oral care advice.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A low-calorie fasting diet can lower inflammation in gums, offering a new way to help fight gum disease alongside traditional treatments.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un régime de jeûne réduit l’inflammation liée aux maladies des gencives
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La maladie des gencives, notamment une forme grave appelée parodontite, touche des millions de personnes dans le monde. Elle provoque une inflammation des gencives et peut entraîner d’autres problèmes de santé comme les maladies cardiaques et le diabète. Les traitements habituels visent à nettoyer les dents et éliminer la plaque, mais les scientifiques ont voulu savoir si l’alimentation pouvait aussi aider.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs du King's College de Londres ont étudié 28 patients en Espagne. La moitié a suivi un régime de jeûne pauvre en calories pendant cinq jours, répété trois fois sur six mois. Ils ont consommé moins de calories que d’habitude : 1 100 calories par jour pendant deux jours, puis 750 calories pendant trois jours. L’autre moitié a continué à manger normalement. Après six mois, le groupe en jeûne avait moins d’inflammation dans le sang et les gencives que le groupe à régime normal.

**Impact** – Cette étude est la première à montrer que le jeûne peut réduire l’inflammation liée spécifiquement aux maladies des gencives. Elle suggère que ce que les gens mangent pourrait être presque aussi important que le brossage des dents pour la santé des gencives. Le régime a fait baisser des marqueurs d’inflammation, ce qui pourrait protéger les gencives et réduire le risque de problèmes de santé graves liés à la maladie des gencives.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent faire des études plus larges pour confirmer ces résultats et savoir comment utiliser les régimes de jeûne en toute sécurité, surtout pour les personnes à risque comme les diabétiques. Ils espèrent étudier comment le jeûne pourrait s’ajouter aux traitements habituels comme le nettoyage dentaire et les conseils d’hygiène bucco-dentaire.

**Une phrase clé** – Un régime de jeûne pauvre en calories peut réduire l’inflammation des gencives, offrant un nouveau moyen de combattre la maladie des gencives en complément des traitements classiques.
articles.personality_title_es: Una dieta de ayuno reduce la inflamación relacionada con la enfermedad de las encías
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La enfermedad de las encías, especialmente una forma grave llamada periodontitis, afecta a millones de personas en todo el mundo. Provoca inflamación en las encías y puede causar otros problemas de salud como enfermedades del corazón y diabetes. Los tratamientos habituales se centran en limpiar los dientes y eliminar la placa, pero los científicos querían saber si la dieta también podría ayudar.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores del King's College de Londres estudiaron a 28 pacientes en España. La mitad siguió una dieta baja en calorías con estilo de ayuno durante cinco días, repetida tres veces en seis meses. Comieron menos calorías de lo normal: 1,100 calorías por día durante dos días, luego 750 calorías durante tres días. La otra mitad comió normalmente. Después de seis meses, el grupo de ayuno mostró menos inflamación en sangre y encías comparado con el grupo que comió normalmente.

**Impacto** – Este estudio es el primero que muestra que el ayuno puede reducir la inflamación relacionada específicamente con la enfermedad de las encías. Sugiere que lo que las personas comen puede ser casi tan importante como cepillarse los dientes para la salud de las encías. La dieta redujo marcadores de inflamación, lo que podría ayudar a proteger las encías y reducir el riesgo de problemas de salud graves relacionados.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren hacer estudios más grandes para confirmar estos resultados y descubrir cómo usar las dietas de ayuno de manera segura, especialmente para personas en riesgo, como quienes tienen diabetes. Esperan explorar cómo el ayuno podría añadirse a los tratamientos habituales para la enfermedad de las encías, como la limpieza dental y consejos de higiene oral.

**Una frase clave** – Una dieta baja en calorías con ayuno puede reducir la inflamación en las encías, ofreciendo una nueva forma de ayudar a combatir la enfermedad de las encías junto con los tratamientos tradicionales.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting showing a stylized human mouth with healthy gums gently cradled by two hands made of soft light, surrounded by subtle representations of a low-calorie diet such as small, simple plates with minimal food portions and a delicate balance scale symbolizing inflammation reduction, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones.
